tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE. The first deliverable is drafted SPEC prose amending §27.6 (staged into spec/27-containers.md, which already exists — this is the rare edit-an-existing-section case, and it should read as the open question CLOSING, not as a new section) plus answered design questions. Then STOP and wait for Myk's word in chat before writing implementation code.

THE QUESTION, as §27.6 states it: a membership query says what is IN a container; a module still needs to say what it EXPORTS — which schemas / renderers / entities are its PUBLIC SURFACE versus internal deltas it needs but does not expose. Membership is decided and built (#132); the manifest is what turns a delta-set into a package with an INTERFACE.

DESIGN QUESTIONS TO ANSWER (each with a reasoned recommendation; none is reserved to Myk unless it turns out to touch capabilities, in which case say so and stop):
1. Is the manifest a SEPARATE artifact, or is it itself deltas inside the container? Strong prior: deltas — everything else in Loam is (trust is data, §8; a Schema is a publishable entity, §21; a container's knobs are claims about it, §27.1). If the manifest is a member delta then the module-version content address (BUILT — `Gateway.freeze`, `src/gateway/container-identity.ts`) covers it for free, and a module version pins its own interface. Argue it or overturn it.

ONE THING THE IDENTITY BUILD LEFT ON THE TABLE FOR YOU, AND THE SHAPE OF ITS ANSWER (Myk, 2026-07-20): a `ModuleVersion` currently holds its members as in-memory Delta objects, which is safe only because that is exactly what `select` already hands a caller and nothing is persisted. The moment a manifest — or anything else — PERSISTS a version, that changes: a stored version must not keep a BYTE COPY of its members, or an erased member (§11/§24.8) would survive inside it, and a stored version becomes somewhere a forgotten delta can hide.

The answer is HASH CONSING, which Loam already gets for free: content addressing IS hash consing, so a delta's id is its structural hash and the canonical instance is the one in the ground. So **a persisted version stores member IDS, never deltas.** That single choice is three things at once — (a) the shared representation, so a version over ten thousand members costs ten thousand addresses rather than ten thousand deltas; (b) the erasure guarantee BY CONSTRUCTION, because a version NAMES its members rather than holding them, so an erased member is simply gone from the ground the version points into and the version can honestly report it missing instead of harboring its bytes; and (c) the composition rule §27.4 already needs, since a version pinning another version is just one more address. Name things by their content address and never hold their bytes: the memory story, the erasure story, and the federation story are one sentence.

Carry that into the manifest's own shape — if the manifest is member deltas (question 1's strong prior), its exports should likewise NAME what they export rather than embed it.
2. What can be exported? §27.6 names schemas, renderers, entities. Is that the closed list? What about a resolver binding (§22) — it is law, and law is exactly what trust-on-load (T31) has to bless.
3. What does an export NAME? A raw delta id is content-addressed and brittle to re-authoring; an entity is stable but unowned. Recommend the shape a consumer writes when they say 'give me this module's Feed schema'.
4. Is a non-exported member READABLE by the consumer, or only unadvertised? Reference-load (§27.3) puts the whole container behind glass the consumer can read through by opt-in (§23.9/§24.2), so 'private' cannot mean unreadable without a read-side capability slice (§24.2's honesty note, explicitly deferred). Recommend what 'internal' honestly means in v1 — likely NOT-ADVERTISED rather than NOT-READABLE — and say so in the prose rather than implying a confidentiality the build does not deliver. This one matters most; the §24 arc has been careful never to promise a boundary it does not enforce.
5. Does the manifest carry a compat statement about what it needs from the HOST (a rhizomatic version, a Loam section)? Or is that a later concern?

WRITE IT so that a reader who has just read §27.6's 'Membership: decided. Manifest: open.' finds the sentence that closes it. Provenance footer per CLAUDE.md.

edges.contract: Trust-on-load asks whether the smallest safe blessing is whole-module or PER-EXPORT. That question is unanswerable until the manifest has decided what an export IS and what it names — T31's recommended shape is a function of T30's answer to its questions 2 and 3.

The manifest is the second policy §27.7 waited for: it is what distinguishes a shippable module from a quarantine pool, and therefore what justifies lifting the hard-wired pool into a knob vector at all.

tickets.title: §27.6 q1: the manifest — where a container stops being a query's output and becomes a package

tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE, and it lands on the CAPABILITY surface — so this PR is Myk's merge (P6) regardless of how clean it is. Deliverable: drafted SPEC prose amending §27.6 plus answered questions, then STOP for Myk in chat.

THE QUESTION, as §27.6 states it: a loaded module carries LAW (schemas, renderers, grants). Inert-by-default (§8/§12) says it binds nothing until blessed; 'install a module' is load + a scoped promotion of its law (§27.3, and §24.4 says promotion-of-law is the ordinary publish path remembering its probation). What is the SMALLEST SAFE BLESSING — the whole module's law at once, or a per-export grant?

Recommendation to argue from, not to assume: PER-EXPORT, because the whole-module blessing is the thing §6 spent the arc refusing — authority never rides in on data, and 'I trust this package' is exactly the ambient grant that makes supply chains dangerous. But argue the cost honestly: per-export blessing means a module with twenty exports is twenty operator acts, and an install flow nobody completes is its own security failure. The interesting middle is a blessing that is per-export in the RECORD (so an audit can say which export bound and who blessed it) while being one operator act at the UI (§24.7's promotion controls at the frame's edge). Say whether that middle is honest or a fig leaf.

QUESTIONS TO ANSWER:
1. Whole-module vs per-export vs the recorded-per-export/one-act middle. Recommend one.
2. Does blessing a module's law inherit §6's two-authority discipline — blessing the CODE and granting its PEN are different keys (§23.3, restated in §24.4)? Presume yes; state where the seam is for a module.
3. What does UNBLESSING look like? A blessed export is a published registration in the operator's own voice (§24.4), so withdrawal is negation — but the module version it came from is immutable. Does unblessing one export leave the rest bound? (This is where per-export earns or loses its keep.)
4. Does a module version pin the law it was blessed WITH — i.e. does upgrading to a new module version RE-REQUIRE blessing? Presume yes and say why: a new version is a new content address, so anything else is a silent authority upgrade.
5. What does the quarantine posture (§24) give here for free? A module loaded by REFERENCE into a container is already the probation story; blessing is promotion out. State the identity explicitly so the two do not drift into parallel mechanisms.

Do NOT decide a reserved '(Myk)' call unilaterally. If any question above turns out to need his sentence rather than a recommendation, name it as such in the PR and leave it open.

edges.contract: The `trust` knob is one of the five in §27.1's vector, and it is the knob that decides whether exclusion may be a PROPERTY or must be a WALL. T32 cannot set that knob correctly until T31 has decided what blessing a container's law actually means.

promote-LAW is the built form of the blessing T31 designs. If T31 recommends a per-export shape, T33 builds that shape rather than inventing a parallel one — so the design must land first or the two mechanisms drift.

tickets.title: §27.6 q3: trust on load — the smallest safe blessing for a module's law

tickets.body:
The move §27.7 named and deliberately deferred until a SECOND policy wanted the same object. T29–T31 supply it: a module version is that second policy, and once it exists the quarantine's hard-wired knobs are an embarrassment rather than a simplification.

BUILD: generalize `quarantine-pool.ts` into a named `Container` carrying the §27.1 knob vector — `{membership, seeding, trust, boundary, identity}` — and re-express the quarantine as ONE PRESET of it (UNTRUSTED · one-way-seeded · live · droppable). `openQuarantine` keeps working, unchanged at the call site, implemented over the general primitive.

THE KNOBS, and where each already exists:
- membership — BUILT (#132): a Term, `QuarantineOptions.membership`, re-evaluated per pulse. Lifts as-is.
- identity — T29: static (freeze → module version) or live (re-evaluate → living container). This is §27.2's ladder, and the lifting is where the two stop being separate code paths.
- seeding — one-way inbound federation from a primary (built), explicit import of a shipped module (new), hand-authored deltas.
- trust — UNTRUSTED (foreign law inert, separate store) vs CURATED. This knob decides whether exclusion may be a PROPERTY or must be a WALL (§27.1's spectrum). Get this one exactly right; §24.10 is the normative reconciliation and it must not drift.

THE TRUST KNOB IS ALSO THE COPY KNOB (Myk, 2026-07-20) — a framing §27.1 implies and never states, and the lifting is where it should be said out loud. Read §27.1's spectrum as a STORAGE claim, not only a security one: a CURATED container is a query over SHARED GROUND — pure pointer arrangement, zero copies, the structural-sharing case, and the reason `select`/`freeze` hand back references into the reactor's snapshot rather than duplicates. An UNTRUSTED container is a SEPARATE ARENA — real byte copies (`openQuarantine` seeds a second backend by `federate(offeredDeltas())`), because discard-with-zero-trace and erasure-evasion resistance are precisely the things a view over shared memory cannot give you. So §24.1's 'you cannot discard a mark' is, underneath, the argument that ISOLATION IS THE ONE THING SHARING CANNOT PROVIDE, and therefore the one thing worth paying a copy for. The design consequence for this ticket is a rule, not a mood: **the Container pays bytes exactly where it needs a wall, and nowhere else** — a curated container that copies is a bug, and an untrusted container that merely filters is a hole. Rail both directions (the CURATED rail below already asserts no content address churns; add the dual — an untrusted container's members are genuinely a separate store, not a view).
- boundary — reference or merge (§27.3). Merge splits by trust: scope-merge (flip the exclusion property, NO re-sign) vs adoption-merge (re-sign with `loam.adoption`, built as promote-outputs, #111).

THE VOCABULARY DOES NOT EXIST YET, and minting it is part of this ticket. §27.1 says a container is itself an ENTITY and its knobs are CLAIMS about it, and that exclusion is 'a claim that container C is excluded' — but nothing under `loam:` or `loam.*` names a container today (grep confirms: no `loam:container`, no `loam.container.*`). So T32 mints reserved vocabulary. That triggers CLAUDE.md's rules in full: the shape must be unambiguously distinct from every prior version so shape-detection stays sufficient, and if it changes bytes older stores already hold it ships a §20 migration step in the SAME PR. Name the vocabulary deliberately and state it in §27.1's prose — it is the at-rest form of the whole knob vector, so a careless name here is one every later section has to live with. Follow the `loam:trust` / `loam:budget` precedent (a declaration re-resolved from live deltas, so changing a knob is a delta and not a restart).

SCOPE-MERGE IS THE NEW OPERATION and the one worth the most care: §27.3 decided it is flipping an exclusion claim on your OWN container, with no re-signing, because authorship never changed. It needs the composable set-algebra rhizomatic 0.6.0 landed (`difference`/`intersect` as first-class Term ops, adopted in T14) — the scope is `union of active containers MINUS the excluded ones`, nestable, which the old depth-1 `select(not(inView(T, id)))` idiom could not express.

RAILS:
- The quarantine preset is behaviorally IDENTICAL to today: re-point `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` at the lifted implementation and it stays green with no assertion softened. In particular §24.8's erasure law (tombstone + purge fan-out, the byte-level composed-scope assertion) must hold through the lifting, untouched.
- A CURATED container admits the property model: exclude it, the primary's read view loses its deltas; re-include it, they return; no delta was re-signed and no content address churned (assert the ids before and after are the SAME).
- An UNTRUSTED container refuses the property model — it is a separate store, and there is no flag that merges it. Assert the refusal, loudly.
- Nested exclusion composes: a container defined RELATIVE to another (difference against difference) resolves correctly. This is the case that was impossible before 0.6.0 and is the reason the algebra was worth adopting.

CARE: this is a refactor of a store-boundary/erasure/federation surface, so it is Myk's merge (P6) even though it is a build ticket. It must ship no behavior change to the quarantine — if it does, that is a finding, not a feature. §24's remaining slices (T33–T35) land ON the lifted primitive, which is why they queue behind it.

edges.contract: T32 refactors quarantine-pool.ts into the general Container. promote-LAW touches the same pool surface, so landing it first would mean writing the promotion path twice — once against the hard-wired pool, once against the lifted primitive.

The resource envelope is a per-CONTAINER budget. Attaching it to the hard-wired quarantine pool would hard-wire one more knob that T32 then has to generalize; after the lifting it is a knob on the vector.

The sequestered frame mounts a renderer against a container's handles and offers promotion controls at its edge. Both the handle scoping and the promotion door are Container surface, so the frame should be built against the lifted primitive.

tickets.title: §27.7: the Container lifting — quarantine becomes one preset of a named primitive

tickets.body:
The second strength of promotion, on §24.9's DESCRIBE list, and the last unbuilt half of §24.3/§24.4. Promote-OUTPUTS is built (#111): a quarantine's FACTS cross by re-signing with a `loam.adoption` record, and it explicitly REFUSES law (a delta declaring a reserved vocabulary is rejected). This ticket builds the door that refusal points at.

WHAT §24.4 RECOMMENDED, and it is the whole design: promotion-of-law needs NO special quarantine machinery. It reuses the ORDINARY publish path verbatim — `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` for a §21 Schema, the §22 binding publish for a resolver, the §23 renderer binding publish for a renderer. The quarantine was the PROBATION; the ordinary publish is the GRADUATION. Blessing is a promotion out of a visible staging area, never a boolean flipped in the dark.

BUILD: `Gateway.promoteLaw(source, deltaId, opts?)` (name it as the codebase wants) which (1) recognizes what KIND of law it is looking at, (2) routes it to that kind's existing publish path under the OPERATOR's authorship — because foreign authorship binds nothing (§8/§12), the operator re-authors to bless, exactly as promote-outputs does for facts — and (3) attaches the quarantine record as PROVENANCE on the blessed thing: a `loam.adoption`-style pointer back to the pool id, the trial, who promoted, when. 'This law was vetted in quarantine before it bound' must be on the record and auditable like any other provenance.

§6'S TWO-AUTHORITY DISCIPLINE IS THE TRAP HERE, and §24.4 flags it: blessing the CODE and granting its PEN are DIFFERENT KEYS (§23.3). Promoting a renderer's law must NOT confer the granted author that lets it write. A rail must prove that: promote a renderer's law out of a pool, then assert it has no pen in the primary until a separate, deliberate grant. If the implementation makes those one act, the ticket has failed even with green tests.

OTHER RAILS:
- Idempotence, matching promote-outputs: promote the same law twice, converge on one registration and one provenance record.
- Erasure holds: a promoted law the operator later erased cannot be silently re-promoted past its tombstone (the same re-mints-the-same-id property #111 relies on).
- The blessed thing actually BINDS: after promotion, a read through the primary's governed door resolves THROUGH the promoted Schema/resolver/renderer. Assert the behavior, not the registration record — a test that only checks the record was written is hollow.
- Provenance survives: `Gateway.adoptions()` (or its law-side equivalent) shows the trail back to the pool.

CARE: capability + federation surface → Myk's merge (P6). If T31 (trust-on-load) recommended a per-export blessing shape, this is where that shape gets built — reconcile with it rather than inventing a parallel mechanism, and say in the PR which way the reconciliation went.

edges.contract: The frame's promotion controls offer BOTH doors — adopt an output (built, #111) and bless the law. The second door has to exist before the frame can put a button on it.

Ordering, not logic: the two are independent in design but the forecast scores them a scope-overlap VETO (both amend spec/24-quarantine.md's provenance and both land tests under the same quarantine surface). At width 1 they run sequentially anyway; this edge makes that explicit rather than leaving a known-conflicting pair nominally concurrent. promote-LAW goes first because it completes a §24.3/§24.4 story already half-built, where the envelope opens a new one.

tickets.title: §24.4: promote-LAW — the graduation is an ordinary publish that remembers its probation

tickets.body:
**RE-WEIGHTED BY T36 (2026-07-21).** This was queued as a hardening slice. Under container-scoped trust (T36) it is one of exactly TWO powers an operator cannot delegate to a child container - the other being erasure reach, already built (#109). A child may admit deltas its parent does not trust; what keeps that safe to HOST is that the operator can still forget (erasure fans out) and still cap the bill (this envelope). Without it, delegated admission means an unbounded bill on someone else's say-so. Build it with that framing in the prose, not as a performance nicety.

§24.9's BUILD list item that slice 1 deferred. §23.9 built the confinement — an operator's bundle runs in a `worker_threads` Worker with a hard timeout and memory `resourceLimits`, bounding HANG, CRASH, and MEMORY. §24.5 says the quarantine needs that worker to run in ITS OWN POOL with ITS OWN budget, and states the hard constraint plainly: the quarantine's budget must not degrade the primary store's doors.

WHERE IT LIVES: `src/gateway/render-worker.ts` is §23.9's implementation and already carries the two comments this ticket acts on — its HONEST SCOPE note (a Worker bounds hang/crash/memory, NOT fs/net; true ocap is 'deferred to §24') and its 'v1 spawns a worker per render (~ms) … a small warm pool is the obvious follow-on'. The slot/concurrency knob below is that follow-on, scoped per pool. `src/gateway/budget.ts` is the neighboring precedent for the shape: door budgets are DATA, one operator-authored declaration re-resolved from the live deltas each request, so raising a quota is a delta and not a restart. Follow that pattern unless there is a stated reason not to — an envelope that needs a restart to tune would be the odd one out in this codebase.

BUILD: a per-quarantine resource envelope — the pool owns a worker budget (timeout, memory, and concurrency/slot count) charged against the QUARANTINE, not the primary. A stranger's app burning its whole envelope must leave the primary's doors answering at full speed.

THE RAIL THAT MATTERS, and it is a real one, not a proxy: with a quarantined workload deliberately exhausting its envelope (spin to timeout, allocate to the memory cap, saturate every slot), a concurrent read through the PRIMARY's door still completes within its normal bound. Make the rail concrete rather than impressionistic: establish the primary's baseline latency with the pool idle, then assert the under-load latency stays within a stated multiple of it (pick the multiple, justify it, and make it generous enough not to flake on CI — the failure this catches is 'the primary blocks until the quarantine's timeout fires', which is an order-of-magnitude effect, not a percentage one). Measure the primary's behavior under quarantine load. A test that merely asserts 'the pool's worker was configured with limits' is HOLLOW — it passes without the isolation existing. §18's door-busy work (T18, 'the door learns to say busy') is the neighboring machinery; reuse rather than parallel it.

OTHER RAILS:
- The envelope is per-POOL: two quarantines cannot borrow each other's budget, and one exhausting itself does not starve the other.
- Exhaustion is LOUD and attributable — the operator learns which pool hit which limit, not a generic failure.
- Dropping a pool releases its envelope (no leak across the drop that §24.1 promises is consequence-free).

THE HONESTY FLAG IS PART OF THE DELIVERABLE. §24.5 states it and the PR must not quietly outgrow it: until the full ocap layer ships (no-fs/no-net — SES-in-worker or isolated-vm, §23.9's named further work), a quarantine that admits rung (d) EFFECTFUL code bounds resource exhaustion but does NOT bound ambient-authority reach — a worker can still reach `node:fs` and the network. This ticket builds the resource half ONLY. Do not let the PR's prose imply the quarantine now safely runs a stranger's effectful app; that promise depends on the ocap slice, which stays deferred and should be re-stated as still-open in §24.5's provenance. Shipping the budget and overstating the confinement would be exactly the theater §24 exists to refuse.

NOT in scope: the ocap confinement itself. That is its own ticket once the SES-vs-isolated-vm choice is made — a dependency conversation, and it needs Myk.

edges.contract: The frame runs a stranger's renderer for real, statefully, against real ground — which is exactly the workload the envelope bounds. Mounting that frame without a budget is the unbounded case §24.5 exists to close.

tickets.title: §24.5: the quarantine resource envelope — a budget that cannot starve the primary's doors

tickets.body:
The vivid case, and the one that makes §24 legible to a person rather than to a spec reader. §23 built most of the path: §23.3 decided a sandboxed renderer writes under a per-renderer granted author into the SANDBOX POOL, never canonical, and §23.9 built the confinement. §24.1 supplied the pool. What §24.7 adds is the FRAME.

BUILD, in the three parts §24.7 names:
FIRST, A CORRECTION TO CARRY INTO THE WORK: §24.7's prose says 'the stock React host (§23.2)', and that phrase does not map onto built code. There is no React host component in Loam. §23.2 is the host CONTRACT — the capability-scoped handles injected into a render function — and the serving path is `serveRouteImpl` in `src/gateway/renderers.ts`, which runs the bundle in the §23.9 worker and returns rendered output. So 'the frame' is chrome the SERVING PATH wraps around a quarantined route's output, not a React component to write. Build it there, and amend §24.7's wording in the same PR so the spec stops describing a host that does not exist.

1. HANDLES SCOPED TO THE POOL — the serving path, mounting a quarantined renderer, injects handles whose writes land IN the quarantine (§23.3's pen-into-pool) and whose reads see `primary-ground ⊎ its-own-writes` (§24.2). The point is that a probationary app is genuinely RUNNING, statefully: the operator watches it DO things, not paint a frozen preview.
2. A VISIBLY-SEQUESTERED FRAME — chrome that says without ambiguity: this is probation; its writes are LIVE HERE and quarantined from your store; promotion is the only crossing. §24.7 is emphatic about the failure mode: the frame must NOT say 'its writes go nowhere.' That would be a LIE — they go into the pool, and that is the whole point of a dry-run. A user must be fooled neither into thinking a probationary face is load-bearing NOR into thinking it is inert.
3. PROMOTION CONTROLS AT THE FRAME'S EDGE — bless the law (§24.4 / T33), adopt an output (§24.3, built #111). The only door out, and it is visible.

RAILS:
- A write from the quarantined renderer lands in the POOL and is absent from the primary, asserted at the store level, not the UI level.
- The app reads its own write back (it is stateful and live, not a preview) — this is the assertion that distinguishes §24.7 from a screenshot.
- Dropping the pool while the frame is mounted is consequence-free, and the frame says so.
- The frame's copy is itself under test for the §24.7 lie: assert the sequestration language does not claim writes go nowhere. (A string test is weak, but the failure mode IS a copy failure, so test the copy.)
- Erasure reaches through the mounted frame (§24.8 holds while a renderer is live, not only at rest).

THE VILLAGE (CLAUDE.md step 8): extend `demos/village/phase-quarantine.mjs` or add a new act so the village RUNS a stranger's face against real ground, writes into the pool, shows the frame, promotes one output, and drops the rest — end-to-end and visible. Update the demonstration ledger in `demos/village/README.md`. This is the act that shows what the whole §24/§27 arc was for; make it ambitious.

CARE: renderer + capability surface → Myk's merge (P6).
tickets.title: §24.7: the sequestered frame — a stranger's app running against your real ground, visibly on probation

tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE, and the largest open design in the arc - capability + federation + tenancy surface, so it is Myk's merge (P6) whatever it looks like. Deliverable: a drafted `spec/28-container-trust.md` plus answered questions, then STOP for Myk in chat.

ORIGIN (Myk, 2026-07-21, in conversation). Asked whether trust is a property of a delta's relationship to the OPERATOR or to each TENANT, the answer turned out to be neither: **trust is a property of a CONTAINER.** A store IS a container (the degenerate one - membership `input`, live, curated), so operator trust is simply the ROOT container's trust; tenants are containers within it, each carrying their own; and containers nest. This collapses four separate open questions into one primitive, and it collects a residual §7 has carried since step 13: 'per-tenant admin chains still mint community-vocabulary grants while constitutional strikes require store standing - revisit with trust-is-data.' Trust-is-data landed (`src/gateway/trust.ts`); the revisit never happened. This is it.

DECIDED ALREADY (Myk, same conversation - do not relitigate, write it down):

1. **Trust is container-scoped.** Not operator-scoped (which is what `trust.ts` builds today: ONE declaration at `loam:trust`, store-wide, operator-authored) and not tenant-scoped. Operator and tenant are two containers at different depths of one tree.

2. **The two axes must not be conflated - this is the heart of the section.** An early draft applied one rule to both and produced a design that would have forced operators toward blanket trust; Myk broke it with the Bob-federates-a-remote-source case. The corrected rule:
   - **ADMISSION** (may these bytes ENTER this container?) - what `loam:trust`'s open/roster/closed actually governs. **Delegates downward freely.** A child container may admit what its parent does not trust. Bob admitting Charlie's deltas into Bob's container puts NO pressure on Alice-the-operator's roster, because Bob's container is a distinct scope and Alice's ground is untouched.
   - **BINDING / EFFECTIVENESS** (does this law have FORCE?) - inert-by-default, §8/§12. **Attenuates.** Law admitted into a child binds within that child AT MOST, and reaches the parent only by PROMOTION (§24.3 adoption-merge, re-sign with `loam.adoption`, built #111).
   Note what the corrected shape IS: a child container is a QUARANTINE relative to its parent - a place where untrusted law may bind, inside and nowhere else, with promotion the only crossing. §24 is this design one level down, which is strong evidence it is right.

3. **The trust knob DETERMINES the boundary knob.** §27.1's 'spectrum keyed on trust' stops being a vibe and becomes a derivation: **a container that admits what its parent does not trust MUST be a WALL (separate store); a container that only narrows its parent's trust MAY be a PROPERTY (a flag over shared ground).** The reason is an escalation that would otherwise arrive by the back door - if a child were merely a SCOPE over the parent's shared ground, a child admitting a stranger would put the stranger's bytes in the parent's store, merely excluded from its default read. The wall is what makes delegated admission safe. This is §24.1's argument and the copy-knob rule (Myk, 2026-07-20) reaching the same place from two directions.

4. **What the operator keeps and cannot delegate.** The honest operator stake is NOT 'I vouch for your sources' but **'I can forget, and I cap the bill'**: (a) ERASURE reaches through every container unconditionally (§24.8, BUILT #109 - `erase` fans out to every attached pool, gated on the tombstone actually landing), so a child can never shelter a byte the operator must forget; (b) the RESOURCE ENVELOPE bounds what a child may spend (§24.5, unbuilt - T34). Those two are why delegated admission does not require delegated liability.

5. **DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-21) — WALL-VS-PROPERTY IS PER-TENANT CONFIGURABLE, AND WALLS ARE THE DEFAULT.** This closes what were questions 4 and 5 (the CLAUDE.md-vs-§7 tenancy drift, and whether tenants mean N backends). Per-tenant configurability is coherent — tenant A a wall and tenant B a property in one instance compose fine on read — so the posture is a deployment choice, not a fixed architecture. **The default is WALL; PROPERTY is opt-in** for tenants you are confident share a trust domain (same org, same team, internal partitioning).

   **The reason is the RATCHET, and §28 must document it explicitly — this is the load-bearing part of the ruling.** The two directions are wildly asymmetric, and the asymmetry inverts the usual start-cheap-and-harden-later instinct:
   - **wall → property** is cheap, in-place, and deliberate: federate the wall's deltas into the root, where root's ADMISSION applies, so the operator must actually admit those authors. That cost is correct — dissolving a wall IS the escalation case, and it should require a decision.
   - **property → wall is NOT ACHIEVABLE IN PLACE**, because the ground is grow-only and the deltas are already commingled. Only three routes exist and two of them are dishonest: (a) copy out then ERASE from root — the tombstone would assert the byte was forgotten while it is deliberately retained in the tenant's new backend, which is §24.8's own prohibition arriving from the inside, and the record would be lying; (b) copy out and keep it in root, excluded — duplication wearing a wall costume, failing the discard-with-zero-trace test that motivated walls at all; (c) RE-PROVISION the store — build a fresh backend holding everything except tenant T and a second holding T, the old store ceasing to exist. Only (c) is honest, and it is an OPERATIONAL MIGRATION, not an operation.
   The general statement §28 should make, because it generalizes past tenancy: **you cannot retroactively achieve isolation over grow-only shared ground.** A wall's value comes from the bytes never having been commingled; once they have been, no in-place act recovers it.
   So walls are the default because the likely direction of travel — 'we thought we did not need isolation, then a customer asked for it' — is the transition that costs a re-provision, while its reverse costs an afternoon.

   **This also resolves the drift, in the right direction:** CLAUDE.md's 'tenant isolation is first-class' becomes the DEFAULT and therefore a true statement; §7's read-lens machinery becomes the opt-in PROPERTY posture. Both true of different deployments, neither wrong, and the stronger promise is the one that holds unless someone opts out. Update CLAUDE.md's standing decision and §7's framing to say so as part of this ticket.

FEASIBILITY - ALREADY INVESTIGATED, DO NOT REDO IT (2026-07-21):
- **The admission half is buildable today, at any depth, with no substrate change.** Admission is NOT a lens: `admitForImpl` (src/gateway/ingest.ts) resolves the policy and returns a plain JS predicate. Walking a container tree to compose admission is ordinary host recursion.
- **The binding half hits a hard substrate ceiling.** `inView` is stratified at PARSE time - `term-json.js`: 'inView is stratified: no inView inside inView.term (SPEC-2 §3.1)'. So a LIVE, in-lens trusted set reaches exactly ONE link. That is why `lawfulStrikersJson` (src/gateway/accounts.ts) is written the way it is, and why §7's residual exists.
- **The workaround is sound**: resolve the container tree in TypeScript and BAKE the flattened result into the lens as a plain `inSet` predicate instead of an `inView`. Works at any depth today. It costs the property rhizomatic#2 was valued for - the lens stops being one live source of truth and becomes a snapshot the host must re-derive when trust moves. Loam already re-resolves trust and budgets from live deltas each request, so the cost is probably small; the risk is a host that forgets to rebuild and silently reads a STALE trusted set.
- Filed as **rhizomatic#27** (enhancement, not a blocker). rhizomatic is FROZEN - do not wait on it, and do not work around it in a forked vocabulary. Build against the bake path; if #27 ever lands, the live path is a later simplification.

SUBSTRATE ANSWER RECEIVED (rhizomatic#27, 2026-07-21) - this supersedes the feasibility paragraph above in two places, do not plan from the older reading:
- **Stratification is staying, and that is now a decided position, not a limitation.** Bounded `inView` nesting was rejected for a reason worth knowing: nesting is SYNTACTIC (depth 2 means literally writing one inside another), while a container tree has DYNAMIC depth - so bounded nesting only ever expresses the statically-known case, which is not ours. A recursive fixed-point op was rejected as Datalog-style recursion that breaks three properties the algebra rests on (O(|delta|) predicate evaluation, decidable subsumption for reactor dispatch, and incremental maintenance under negation-aware masks).
- **There are TWO sanctioned routes for recursive trust, not one.** SPEC-2 §3 already says general cross-delta logic belongs at L7, where a derived author computes it and asserts it as a delta that then becomes selectable. So: **(a) host-flatten to `inSet`** - zero lag, re-resolved per request, no provenance; or **(b) a derived author emits the closure as signed deltas and the lens does a plain depth-1 `inView` over those** - any depth, cycles handled once inside the function, and real provenance (`rhizomatic.derived.from` pins the input view's canonical hash, `explain` traces function -> input snapshot -> deltas -> authors, pure derivations replay-verifiable at Conformance Level 4).
- **DECIDED for Loam: (a) on the binding path, (b) alongside as AUDIT.** Route (b) carries ONE DERIVATION HOP OF LAG (grant lands -> materialization -> derivation fires -> closure ingests -> lens updates), so a revoked grant would still bind for one cascade. §7 already states and tests the opposite - 'revoking a grant un-binds its author's strikes on the very next read' - so putting binding on (b) would silently REGRESS a stated guarantee, and the regression is invisible because a one-cascade-stale trusted set looks well-formed. Use (a) for binding. Use (b) for the audit artifact, because 'show me why this author was trusted, and let me verify it myself' is a real tenancy requirement that (a) cannot answer at any price.
- **QUESTION 2 IS ANSWERED - the staleness worry was overstated when this ticket was written.** The 'host forgets and reads a stale set' failure mode exists ONLY IF THE HOST CACHES the flattened set. Compute per request and never store it, and staleness is structurally impossible rather than merely detectable. **The guard is architectural: no cache to go stale.** Do not add a staleness-detection mechanism; add the absence of a cache, and say so in §28's prose so nobody helpfully introduces one later.
- **`expand.reading` (SPEC-2 §4.5) - checked, and the walls-by-default ruling defuses it for the default posture.** rhizomatic flagged that `reading` lives in the GATHER BODY, so sibling lenses over one HyperSchema necessarily share the child's reading - meaning 'tenant A and tenant B read embedded posts differently' is not expressible by giving them different lenses. But a WALL tenant has its own store, own registrations, own gather bodies, so they were never sharing a body. The constraint bites only the PROPERTY posture, where it becomes a stated limit: property-tenants share child readings, and needing per-tenant child readings is itself a reason to choose a wall. §28 should say that. (Confirmation requested from the substrate side; if wall-tenants can still end up sharing a body, this paragraph is wrong and the `reading: {hole}` escape becomes a prerequisite rather than a maybe.)
- Loam is pinned at rhizomatic **0.8.0** and the mandatory 0.8 migration is discharged (expands name their child's reading; the §20 step for legacy bodies shipped with its rails). At the 0.9.0 upgrade, parsing goes FAIL-CLOSED ON UNKNOWN KEYS, not just unknown tags - a misspelled or leftover key that used to be dropped silently will throw by name. Loam generates terms in at least four places (governed gather body, lawful-strikers pred, trust roster pred, container membership terms); do a deliberate pass over every generated term at upgrade rather than trusting the suite to surface it.

QUESTIONS THIS TICKET MUST ANSWER:
1. Where does a container's trust declaration LIVE, and who may author it? Today `loam:trust` is one entity, store-wide, operator-only. Per-container needs a per-container declaration with a per-container lawful-author rule - and that rule is the recursion the bake path flattens. Say exactly how a child's admin standing is established without inView recursion.
2. Does the bake path need a STALENESS guard? A host that forgets to re-derive gets a silently stale trusted set with no error - an invisible failure mode, which is the kind this codebase usually refuses. Recommend a discipline (re-derive per request, as trust.ts and budget.ts do) and say whether it should be ENFORCED rather than documented.
3. What happens to §7's tenant machinery? It currently survives as 'vocabulary for author-communities and read lenses, not write fences.' Under this design a tenant becomes a real container with real trust. Is the existing vocabulary reused, or does it become a compatibility shim? A §20 migration question hides in this answer.

4. How deep may the tree go, and is depth bounded? §27.4 already rules that LIVE containers stay a TREE (drop-safety) and frozen ones may be a pinned DAG. Confirm that rule covers 'and so on' nesting, or name where it does not.

All four questions above are open and yours to RECOMMEND on — none is reserved to Myk. (The original questions 4 and 5 — the CLAUDE.md-vs-§7 tenancy drift, and whether tenants mean N backends — were the reserved ones; both are now answered by DECIDED item 5 above, which is why this list runs 1-4.)

edges.contract: T32 mints the container vocabulary PERMANENTLY. Under container-scoped trust every container carries its own trust declaration, so the at-rest shape T32 fixes must have room for it. Landing T32 first would mint a vocabulary that has to be migrated the moment this design lands - and it is a reserved-vocabulary mint, so that migration is expensive and public.

T34 stops being a hardening nicety and becomes load-bearing: the resource envelope is one of the two powers an operator CANNOT delegate (the other, erasure reach, is already built in #109). It is what makes hosting a delegated-trust container safe at all. T36 states that; T34 builds it.

tickets.title: Trust is container-scoped - admission delegates down, effectiveness attenuates (new SPEC §28)

tickets.body:
SURFACED while drafting §28 (2026-07-21), when Myk asked whether §28's no-cache rule commits Loam to gradual performance degradation. It does not - but the answer turned up a degradation that ALREADY EXISTS and predates §28 entirely.

THE FINDING. `lawfulSnapshot` (src/gateway/registration.ts) is a FULL SCAN of the reactor snapshot, materialized into a new DeltaSet:

    return DeltaSet.from([...reactor.snapshot()].filter((d) => d.claims.author === operator));

And `readTrustPolicy` (src/gateway/trust.ts) calls it TWICE per invocation - once inside `lawfulNegated` (which also builds a Set of every lawful delta id) and once again to iterate looking for the handful of deltas that point at `loam:trust`. So resolving the trust policy is O(N) with two full passes, where N is EVERY DELTA IN THE STORE, and it runs per request (trust.ts and budget.ts both advertise 're-resolved from the live deltas each request' as a feature - and it IS one; the freshness is correct, the cost is not).

The ground is grow-only. N only ever increases. So the trust path gets monotonically slower for the life of the store, to find a declaration set that is typically one delta. The same shape almost certainly affects the other constitutional readers built on `lawfulSnapshot`/`lawfulNegated` - `readRegistrations`, `readBindingDefinitions`, `grantHeld`, budget resolution - so measure before assuming this is only trust.

WHY IT MATTERS NOW. §28 makes the trusted set a walk over the CONTAINER TREE rather than a single root lookup, so this per-request O(N) gets multiplied by tree depth. Depth is small (root -> tenant -> maybe a quarantine), so the multiplier is ~3 rather than alarming - but multiplying a cost that already grows without bound is the wrong direction, and §28 would take the blame for a problem it merely made visible.

THE FIX IS AN INDEX, NOT A CACHE - and the distinction is the whole ticket. §28 prohibits caching the FLATTENED TRUSTED SET, because that is a correctness-critical derived value which, if stored, goes stale silently and produces a well-formed wrong answer (a revoked author still binding). That prohibition says nothing about making the COMPUTATION fast. An index by (target entity, context) is a different object: maintained on ingest rather than stored by a caller who might forget to refresh, and it changes the cost of finding the declarations without changing what is computed from them. Materializations are already exactly this kind of structure; this is asking for the same discipline one layer down.

BUILD (recommendation, not prescribed): give the reactor-side lookup a by-entity/by-context path so a constitutional reader asks for 'deltas pointing at loam:trust' instead of scanning and filtering. If rhizomatic already exposes such a path, use it and this is a small refactor; if it does not, this may be a substrate conversation (rhizomatic is FROZEN - file, do not fork). Establish which BEFORE designing, and record the answer in the ticket.

RAILS - and the first one is the point of the ticket:
- A LATENCY rail with a growing store: resolve the trust policy against a store of N deltas and again at 10N, where the number of trust declarations is unchanged. The cost must not grow proportionally to N. Assert the relationship, not an absolute number, so the rail survives a fast machine.
- Freshness is PRESERVED: every existing guarantee holds unchanged - a roster edit binds on the next request, and §7's 'revoking a grant un-binds its author's strikes on the very next read' still holds. This ticket must not buy speed with staleness; that trade is exactly what §28 forbids.
- The other constitutional readers keep their current semantics exactly - `test/gateway/` should stay green with no assertion softened.

NOT IN SCOPE: any cache of a resolved policy or trusted set. If profiling later shows an index is insufficient, that is a design change needing its own invalidation story (§28.6 says so), not a local optimization someone adds in this ticket.
tickets.title: Constitutional reads scan the whole store, every request - index them before the tree multiplies it

tickets.body:
CONFIRMED BY RUNNING IT (2026-07-21). Not a theory - `test/gateway/membership-negation-probe.test.ts` reproduces it and is committed SKIPPED. **Un-skipping that file is step one of this ticket**; the rail already exists and does not pass.

WHAT HAPPENS. Append a claim, retract it with an ordinary negation delta, then seed a quarantine with a membership Term that selects the claim but not the negation (trivially common - the probe uses `hasPointer{context: height}`, and a negation points at a DELTA, not a context, so it is never a member). In the pool the claim reads as LIVE. The retraction did not survive the seeding edge.

WHY. rhizomatic's `negated(d, D)` ranges over the OPERAND SET (SPEC-2 §4.3): suppression is a property of the set being evaluated, not of the delta. Filter a set and keep a target while dropping its negation, and the target comes back un-suppressed. The substrate has this pinned as a conformance vector - `select-then-mask-scopes-to-operand` in `eval-basic.json`, 'the negation d4 is excluded by the select, so nothing in the subset is suppressed.' It is correct substrate behavior; Loam's seeding edge is what is wrong.

Surfaced by the rhizomatic side on issue #27, where it was raised about a FUTURE property->wall migration (§28.4). Checking whether it applied to shipped code found that it does: `QuarantineOptions.membership` (T15, [#132](https://github.com/bombadil-labs/loam/pull/132)) is a filter of exactly that shape.

WHAT IS NOT BROKEN, so the fix does not flail: §24.8 ERASURE is intact and was handled deliberately - `quarantine-pool.ts` passes TOMBSTONES across the seeding edge unconditionally (`admit: (d) => isTombstone(d.claims) || admit(d)`), so a forgotten byte is still forgotten in every pool, and `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` pins it byte-for-byte. The hole is ORDINARY NEGATION - retraction, revocation, any `negates` pointer that is not a tombstone. The existing code proves the shape of the fix is already understood; it was simply applied to one class of negation and not the other.

FIX (recommendation): the seeding edge must carry the NEGATION CLOSURE of whatever it admits. A delta crosses if the membership Term selects it OR it negates something that crossed - transitively, since negating a negation revives (the algebra `lawfulNegated` already walks). Content addressing keeps that chain acyclic, and `lawfulNegated` memoizes it, so the closure is cheap and the existing traversal can likely be reused rather than re-implemented.

Decide and state one thing explicitly: the closure admits deltas the membership Term did NOT select, so a pool's contents are no longer exactly `select(term)`. That is correct - a scope may narrow what you SEE but must never resurrect what was struck - but it makes membership 'the Term plus its negation closure,' and §27.6 currently says a container's members ARE the Term's dset. Amend that sentence rather than leaving the two definitions to drift.

RAILS:
- Un-skip the probe: a claim retracted in the primary must not read as live in a pool seeded by a Term that excludes its negation.
- TRANSITIVE: negation-of-a-negation revives the claim, and that revival must survive the edge too (strike, counter-strike, seed, read - the claim is live in the pool because it is live in the primary, not by accident of filtering).
- The closure does not LEAK: a negation whose target never crossed must not drag the target in. The closure follows negations of admitted deltas, not the reverse.
- Live-follow keeps it: a retraction landing in the primary AFTER seeding reaches the pool on the next pulse (the probe covers the seed path; this covers `reseed`).
- §24.8 unchanged - `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` stays green with no assertion softened. This ticket must not disturb the erasure path.
- `select()` ITSELF is unchanged and still returns exactly the Term's dset. The closure belongs to the SEEDING EDGE, not to the reading surface - a caller asking 'what does this Term select' must still get that answer.

Also check, before assuming the blast radius is one function: every other place Loam filters a delta-set into a narrower one. `offeredDeltas`/`offeredLens` is the obvious sibling - a federation peer pulling through an offered lens could receive a claim without its retraction, which would be the same bug pointed outward at a stranger's store. If it is, this ticket covers both and says so.

THIRD SITE, FOUND IN THE FALLOUT SWEEP (2026-07-21): **`Gateway.freeze` has the same shape** - `freezeMembers(selectImpl(this, term))` captures exactly the Term's dset with no closure (src/gateway/gateway.ts, container-identity.ts; T29, landed the same day). LATENT rather than live, because nothing consumes a ModuleVersion yet - but a module version exists precisely to be SHIPPED and loaded elsewhere, so it becomes a real bug the moment T30 or T32 builds on it, and it is cheaper to fix while nothing depends on the addresses.

It needs a DECISION rather than a silent patch, because the closure changes what `freeze` returns and therefore the content address: T29's rail asserting exact membership (`test/gateway/container-identity.test.ts`) would have to change with it. Recommendation: YES, carry the closure - a version that ships a claim without its retraction hands its consumer a withdrawn claim reading as live, which is the whole failure this ticket is about. Note that the address consequence is CORRECT, not a wart: two stores freezing the same Term where one holds a retraction and one does not get different addresses, because they are genuinely different sets, and the address is doing its job by saying so. Update the T29 rails to expect the closure rather than working around it.

edges.contract: T32 lifts the quarantine pool into the general Container, and every container is seeded through a membership filter. Landing the lifting before this fix generalizes the hole to every posture instead of one - and §28's property->wall path has the same shape, so the negation-closure rule wants to exist before more code depends on the filter.

tickets.title: A membership-scoped seeding edge drops the negation closure - retracted claims come back alive in the pool

tickets.body:
CONFIRMED BY RUNNING IT (2026-07-21). `test/gateway/promote-survival-probe.test.ts` reproduces it. Found during the T38 fallout sweep - same family, DIFFERENT REMEDY, which is why it is its own ticket.

WHAT HAPPENS. `promoteImpl` (src/gateway/adopt.ts) gates on presence:

    const src = source.reactor.get(deltaId);
    if (src === undefined) throw new Error(`nothing to promote: ...`);

`reactor.get` says the delta EXISTS in the pool. It says nothing about whether it SURVIVES there - whether something in the pool struck it. Meanwhile `promotionRefusal` correctly refuses to promote a NEGATION ('a retraction is the operator's own §14 act, never an adopted output').

Each rule is right alone; together they leave a hole. The retraction cannot cross, but its target can. So a sandboxed app that produces an interpretation and then WITHDRAWS it leaves an output the operator can still adopt - and promotion RE-SIGNS, so the withdrawn claim enters the primary in the OPERATOR'S OWN VOICE with full force. The record then shows the operator asserting something its author had already taken back, with a `loam.adoption` trail that makes it look properly sourced.

WHY THIS IS WORSE THAN T38. T38's seeding edge gave a POOL a wrong reading of the primary - bad, but contained behind glass and dropped with the pool. This puts a resurrected claim into CANONICAL history under the operator's signature, where §11 erasure is the only way back out. Promotion is the one door out of the quarantine (§24.9's 'promotion is the only crossing'), so it is exactly the door that must not pass something the pool had already struck.

FIX (recommendation): `promoteImpl` refuses a source delta that does not SURVIVE in the source. Reuse the pool's own negation reading rather than inventing a second notion of survival - whatever `lawfulNegated`/the reactor already answers for 'is this struck', ask that. The refusal must NAME the retraction (the codebase's refusals say why), and it belongs beside the existing `promotionRefusal` checks so the whole promotion gate reads in one place.

Decide one thing explicitly and write it into §24.3: does a LATER retraction in the pool retroactively affect an ALREADY-adopted delta? Recommendation: NO, and say so - the adoption was a lawful operator act at the time, the adopted delta is the operator's own claim now, and withdrawing it is the operator's own §14 retraction rather than an echo of the pool's. Anything else would make canonical history mutable by a sandbox, which is the property §24 exists to deny. But it must be STATED, because the alternative reading is defensible and silence would leave it to whoever implements next.

RAILS:
- The probe: a pool output struck inside the pool cannot be promoted; the refusal names the retraction.
- TRANSITIVE: strike, then counter-strike (which revives), and promotion SUCCEEDS again - survival is the live reading, not 'was ever negated'.
- An already-adopted delta is unaffected by a retraction landing in the pool afterwards (the decision above, railed).
- Unchanged: every existing promotion path stays green - `test/gateway/promotion.test.ts` with no assertion softened, idempotence and the erasure interaction (#111) intact.

CARE: capability + federation surface, and it changes what enters canonical history -> Myk's merge (P6).
tickets.title: Promotion checks PRESENCE, not SURVIVAL - a retracted pool output is adopted in the operator's own voice

tickets.body:
AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle 'narrowing'. Two CONFIRMED holes the T38 fix did not close. T38 shipped an incomplete fix; this finishes it.

(1) **THE `admit` BRANCH IS UNFIXED.** `src/gateway/quarantine-pool.ts:86` - `if (opts.membership === undefined) return base === undefined ? undefined : base;` returns the caller's raw predicate. T38 wrapped only the `membership` Term branch (:92). Only tombstones get the unconditional pass (:101); ordinary negations do not. So `openQuarantine({ admit: d => d.claims.author === stranger })` admits a claim the operator had STRUCK on the primary, without its retraction, and it binds live in the pool. Compounded by T39 (promote checks presence not survival), that revived claim is adoptable into canonical history in the operator's voice. The comment block at :70-74 asserts the property for BOTH knobs while one implements it - fix the comment too, it is currently false.

(2) **THE FEDERATION INBOUND DOOR DROPS NEGATIONS, DEFEATING THE OUTBOUND FIX END-TO-END.** `offeredDeltasImpl` now ships the closure outbound. The receiver applies a per-delta `admit` with no closure at all (`ingest.ts:243` via `admitForImpl` :109 roster mode, and `federation/pull.ts:73`). Roster mode is `author === operator || roster.has(author)` - purely authorship-scoped - so a negation authored by anyone off the roster is rejected while its target is admitted.

CONCRETE FAILURE: you roster `alice` to pull her posts from peer P. On P, the moderator (P's operator, or a write-granted author - neither on YOUR roster) retracted one of Alice's posts. P correctly offers both. Your door admits the post, rejects the retraction, and your GraphQL surface serves a retracted post as live - publicly, if that lens is open. The peer cannot tell.

THE REMEDY DIFFERS FROM `withNegationClosure` and this is the design point: the closure must run over the OFFERED BATCH, not the local snapshot, because those negations are not local yet. Decide whether admitting a negation whose author the roster excludes is correct - recommendation YES for negations specifically, since refusing them cannot protect you (it only makes you wrong about what survives), but state the reasoning; it is a genuine widening of what a roster admits.

Also: `select`/`watch` return the raw dset by design, making the closure a CALLER obligation on a public API - and `freeze` is exactly the caller that forgot. Add a doc-comment on both pointing at hazard H1.

RAILS: extend `test/gateway/negation-closure.test.ts` - the admit-predicate branch preserves suppression; a rostered pull carries an off-roster retraction; the batch-scoped closure does not leak.

CARE: federation surface -> Myk's merge (P6).
tickets.title: Finish the negation closure - the admit branch and the federation INBOUND door were both missed

tickets.body:
AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle 'erasure reach'. CONFIRMED EVASION, and the spec already knows its name.

`QuarantineOptions.backend` accepts any `StoreBackend` ('pass a durable backend for a long-running quarantine', `quarantine-pool.ts:39`). The pool is tracked ONLY in `gw.quarantinePools`, an in-process `Set<Gateway>` (:109), and `eraseImpl` fans out over exactly that Set (`erase.ts:254`). Nothing persists the pool's existence - the auditor grepped for a pool registry or boot re-attachment and found none.

SCENARIO: operator opens a quarantine over `new SqliteBackend('pool.db')`, seeded from P. Process exits. New process boots P. Operator erases `d`. `gw.quarantinePools` is empty, the fan-out loop runs ZERO times, `erase` returns success - and `d`'s bytes sit in `pool.db` forever, inside the operator's own walls.

This is verbatim the premortem finding §24.8 records AND TURNS INTO A BUILD RULE: 'a durable quarantine must be REGISTERED (a record the primary reads at boot, re-attaching the pool to the fan-out) or it may not be durable; an unregistered durable pool is an erasure-evasion channel and the erase operation must refuse to report completeness it cannot deliver.' NEITHER HALF IS IMPLEMENTED - no registration, and no refusal. `test/gateway/erasure-fanout.test.ts` rails five behaviors; none survives a restart, because all five use in-process handles.

SECOND, RELATED (same ticket - the fan-out's recovery): a partially-failed fan-out CANNOT BE RETRIED. `eraseImpl` iterates pools sequentially; the first `eraseReplica` that throws aborts the loop, so every pool after it never receives the tombstone, and `erase` rejects. That much is the documented best-effort-and-loud contract. But the primary already purged and re-seated, so a second `Gateway.erase(id)` hits `erase.ts:227` - 'nothing to erase' - and throws. The rejection also never names which pools were reached. The operator holds a known-incomplete erasure with the public remedy closed. A retry-safe `erase` (tolerating an already-purged target when a lawful tombstone for it stands, and reporting per-pool outcomes) closes this without weakening loudness.

RAILS: a durable pool re-attaches at boot and receives a later erasure (assert at the BYTE level in the pool's backend); an unregistered durable pool makes `erase` REFUSE to report completeness; a fan-out that fails partway can be retried to completion and reports per-pool outcomes.

CARE: erasure + federation surface -> Myk's merge (P6). This is the largest architectural hole the audit found.
tickets.title: A durable quarantine pool escapes the erasure fan-out across a restart - §24.8's own build rule is unbuilt

tickets.body:
AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle 'erasure reach'. Three retention sites; the first also carries a plain non-erasure bug.

(1) **`watch()` CHANNELS ARE NEVER RE-SEATED - CONFIRMED, and this is two bugs.** `reads.ts:266` registers its entity-stream channel with `gw.channels.add(channel)`, so `reseat()` closes it and parked readers are deliberately woken. `watchImpl` creates its Channel at `ingest.ts:189` and NEVER adds it to `gw.channels`. So: (a) the channel's coalescing buffer may hold a pending `Delta[]` including the erased delta - full content and signature - and `reseat()` does not close it, so a slow reader consuming after the purge receives erased bytes from a live Loam API; and (b) its `subscribeRaw` was registered on the OLD reactor, which `reseat()` replaces (`gateway.ts:635`), so the watcher SILENTLY FREEZES on its pre-erase membership forever - no `done`, no update, no way to notice. (b) is a plain correctness bug independent of erasure, and it contradicts `erase.ts:210`'s promise that 'live subscriptions re-attach exactly as they do after a schema evolution'. Fix is likely one line - register the channel - but rail both halves.

(2) **THE ESM CACHE RETAINS ERASED SOURCE - CONFIRMED.** `esm.ts:22` caches module namespaces in a MODULE-LEVEL Map keyed by content address, loading via `data:text/javascript;base64,...` which Node's ESM registry retains for the process lifetime with no eviction. The cache is process-global, shared across the primary AND every quarantine pool. Erase a §22 resolver or §23 renderer delta: the backend purges, `reseat()` explicitly clears `resolverMemo` (`gateway.ts:628`) - and nothing clears the ESM cache or Node's registry. The erased source stays resident and executable, on the normal path (`preloadResolvers` runs on every rebind). Severity is capped because the byte is code rather than a subject's record, but §11's rule is about the byte.

(3) **A HELD `ModuleVersion` RETAINS DELTAS - PLAUSIBLE.** `Gateway.freeze` returns `readonly members: readonly Delta[]` - real Delta objects, explicitly 'captured at freeze time and never re-evaluated' (`container-identity.ts`). No erasure invalidates one and the fan-out does not enumerate them. Caller-held rather than gateway-held, so weaker - but it is a byte-retaining structure the erasure story has not accounted for, and T38 already flags freeze for a closure fix. Decide whether a ModuleVersion should hold ids rather than deltas (SPEC §27.8 already says a PERSISTED version must name rather than hold; this is the in-memory dual).

RAILS: erase, then assert a parked `watch` reader cannot receive the erased bytes and that the watcher is closed or updated rather than frozen; erase a resolver and assert its source is not still loadable from cache.

CARE: erasure surface -> Myk's merge (P6).
tickets.title: In-memory structures retain erased bytes - watch channels, the ESM cache, and held ModuleVersions

tickets.body:
AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle 'presence vs survival'. CONFIRMED. `adopt.ts` is the ONLY file in src/ that reads a constitutional vocabulary and does not appear in a grep for `negated|struck|lawfulNegated|surviving` - structurally conspicuous beside eight disciplined siblings.

`readAdoptions` (`src/gateway/adopt.ts:101-130`, loop head :103) iterates `reactor.snapshot()` filtering on operator authorship and `isAdoption(...)`, and never applies `lawfulNegated`. Three consequences:

(1) **THE AUDIT TRAIL LIES.** `Gateway.adoptions()` is documented as the visible trail from a canonical value back to the quarantine that produced it, 'for audit and review (§27)'. A provenance record the operator explicitly struck keeps appearing in it. §24.3's premise is that this trail is authoritative.

(2) **PROMOTION SILENTLY NO-OPS.** `promoteImpl` builds `trail` from `gw.adoptions()` (:168), then at :198 short-circuits when `trail.get(deltaId) === adopted.id && gw.reactor.get(adopted.id) !== undefined` - both halves presence checks over a stale trail. Operator promotes P->A with record R, then negates R (withdrawing the provenance, keeping the value). Re-promoting P hits this branch, RETURNS `{ promoted: A }`, and lands NOTHING - no new record. The canonical value lives in history with no surviving provenance and promotion refuses to re-establish it, permanently, while telling the caller it succeeded. **This is the same honesty defect PR #151 fixed for `publish` ('publish answers its outcome'), reappearing in `promote`** - which by CLAUDE.md's own P7 rule makes it a defense rather than a third fix (see the hazard file).

(3) Citation rewrite (:172-179) rides the same stale trail - lower severity, since a citation is about resolvability, but a withdrawn adoption still steers what new canonical deltas point at.

FIX: `const negated = lawfulNegated(reactor, operator)` at the top of `readAdoptions` and `continue` on `negated(d.id)`. That one change also fixes the promote no-op, since the trail then correctly forgets and promotion re-lands a fresh record.

RAILS: a struck adoption record does not appear in `adoptions()`; re-promoting after striking the record LANDS A NEW RECORD and says so; `test/gateway/promotion.test.ts` currently has no negated-adoption case, so nothing pins any of this.

CARE: promotion surface -> Myk's merge (P6).
tickets.title: readAdoptions never checks survival - the audit trail lies and promote silently no-ops

tickets.body:
AUDIT of #152/#153/#154 (2026-07-21), finding F1. CONFIRMED, byte-verified. **Highest priority of the audit batch.**

`src/gateway/container-identity.ts:50` embeds LITERAL U+0000 bytes as the domain separator rather than `\u0000` escapes. Two consequences:

(1) **The file is BINARY to git.** `.gitattributes` is `* text=auto`; a NUL flips detection. `git diff 979ea6e~1..979ea6e -- src/gateway/container-identity.ts` reports `Bin 0 -> 4015 bytes, 0 insertions(+), 0 deletions(-)`. The KEYSTONE file of #152 — the one defining module-version identity — passed review **with no visible diff at all**.

(2) **Every `ModuleVersion.id` ever minted is hostage to a control character surviving a round-trip.** Prettier, an editor save, a lint autofix, a copy-paste, a git filter, a future codegen pass — any silent normalization changes the hash preimage and re-mints every module-version address in existence. §27.2's promise ('two consumers who froze the same members agree without coordinating') dies SILENTLY; nothing errors.

FIX: write the separator as `\u0000` escapes in the template literal. Byte-identical output, plain-text file, reviewable diff.

THE RAIL THAT MUST COME WITH IT: pin `addressOf` over a FIXED member set to a LITERAL expected hash. Today's rails all compare two computed ids to each other, so they agree under ANY separator — they cannot see this class at all. A literal-hash rail fails loudly if the preimage ever moves.

NOTE (upstream precedent): ADLC hit this exact class in its own source and fixed it — `d566e95 fix: remove raw control bytes from source and guard against their return (#218)`. Worth reading their guard for the shape of a repo-wide check.

SWEEP: `grep -rlP '\x00' src/ test/` — `src/gateway/resolvers.ts` is known to hold a deliberate NUL in its memo key and has the same exposure.
tickets.title: container-identity.ts holds raw NUL bytes — its PR merged as a zero-line binary diff

tickets.body:
AUDIT of #152/#153/#154 (2026-07-21), findings F2 + F3. My own T40 fix, reviewed. F2 CONFIRMED, F3 PLAUSIBLE.

**F2 — the VACUUM can make a store UN-OPENABLE.** `src/store/sqlite.ts:71-72` runs `VACUUM` in the CONSTRUCTOR when `freelist_count > 0`. VACUUM needs an exclusive write lock over the whole file. With a second handle holding a read transaction — the exact scenario the purge path 120 lines below probes and handles — it waits out `busy_timeout` and throws SYNCHRONOUSLY out of `new SqliteBackend(path)`. `src/cli/cli.ts:113, 217, 294, 385, 423` all construct it bare. **Before this change, opening a store took no exclusive lock at all.** The file's own header pins the contract this broke: WAL + busy_timeout is 'the multi-handle provision', and 'the methods are async so every failure arrives as a rejected promise'. The constructor is not async and has no guard. Secondary: VACUUM rewrites the entire file and needs ~2x its size free, so first open of a large store blocks for its full size on a path nothing treats as expensive.

FIX: wrap in try/catch. **The store must OPEN; the scrub is best-effort-and-REPORTED, not open-blocking.** Report the deferral rather than swallowing it (do not repeat H7 while fixing H7).

**F3 — the constructor VACUUM has no checkpoint.** In WAL mode VACUUM's rebuilt pages land in `store.db-wal`; the main file keeps the inherited plaintext freelist until a checkpoint folds it in. The purge path deliberately runs `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` AND checks `busy` for exactly this reason. The constructor does neither, so a process killed before `close()` leaves the store in §11 violation while the code believes it healed. FIX: follow the VACUUM with the same CHECKED checkpoint. PROBE to confirm: construct over a store with inherited freelist, do NOT close, then `strings store.db`.
tickets.title: The sqlite constructor VACUUM can wedge a store closed, and may not reach the main file

tickets.body:
AUDIT of #152/#153/#154 (2026-07-21), finding F4. CONFIRMED. Also my own T40 fix.

`src/store/archive.ts:187-189`:

    try { namesByFan.set(fan, readdirSync(join(this.root, fan))); }
    catch { namesByFan.set(fan, []); }

The comment justifies this as ENOENT tolerance ('a fan that vanishes between listing and reading'), but the catch is UNQUALIFIED — EACCES, EMFILE, ENOTDIR all land here too. An unreadable fan contributes no straggler names, its `.tmp` files survive the purge, and `purge` returns its count with no signal. That is §11's byte-removal promise reported as kept when it was not: **hazard H7 verbatim**.

The tell is the asymmetry INSIDE ONE TICKET: the sqlite sibling in the same PR refuses loudly on an incomplete erasure, with a paragraph explaining why silence is not permitted. This path went the other way, in the same change, written the same hour.

FIX: rethrow unless `(err as NodeJS.ErrnoException).code === 'ENOENT'`.

RAIL: make a fan unreadable (chmod, or a file where a directory is expected) and assert `purge` REFUSES rather than returning a count. Skip on platforms where the permission cannot be arranged, and say so in the test rather than silently passing.
tickets.title: archive purge swallows every readdir error, reporting a completeness it did not deliver

tickets.body:
AUDIT of #152/#153/#154 (2026-07-21), finding F5. CONFIRMED. Performance, not correctness.

`src/gateway/ingest.ts:130` builds `const byId = new Map([...gw.reactor.snapshot()].map((d) => [d.id, d]))` — an O(|store|) spread plus Map allocation on EVERY invocation, across three hot paths: every federation request (`offeredDeltasImpl`), every quarantine pulse and reseed (`memberAdmit`), and every `freeze`. Before this change `offeredDeltasImpl` under a lens cost O(|selected|); it is now O(|store|) regardless of how narrow the lens is.

And the Map buys NOTHING: `Reactor.get(id)` is already the O(1) lookup, and `test/gateway/narrowing.ts:44` uses exactly that.

FIX: delete `byId`; call `gw.reactor.get(negationId)`. Same purge semantics (a purged negation still returns `undefined`), one line shorter.

ALSO, latent and worth a comment rather than a change: the closure is drawn from the LOCAL SNAPSHOT, so a caller passing a batch not derived from this store would silently under-close. All three current callers are snapshot-derived. T43's inbound-federation fix is exactly the case where that stops being true — its closure must run over the OFFERED BATCH — so note the distinction where a future caller will read it.
tickets.title: withNegationClosure materializes the whole store per call on three hot paths

tickets.body:
AUDIT of #152/#153/#154 (2026-07-21), finding F6. CONFIRMED. Three parts, one root: a behavior change landed without its documentation or its rails.

`Gateway.freeze` now returns `select(term) union negation-closure`. That is the RIGHT call and the gateway comment argues it well. But:

(1) **THE SPEC WAS NOT TOLD.** `spec/27-containers.md` contains no occurrence of 'closure', 'negation', 'struck', or 'retract' ANYWHERE. §27.2's provenance describes `freeze` as evaluating the Term once and naming the result, with no hint that members EXCEED the Term's dset. Per CLAUDE.md the spec is the record of what IS, and it now under-describes the built behavior on its keystone property. Amend §27.2 to state that a version carries the closure — and that two stores with the same members but different retraction states get DIFFERENT (correct) addresses. Also fix `container-identity.ts:56`'s 'freeze IS select plus an address', which is about the Term DOOR but will be read as members.

(2) **THE VILLAGE ENCODES THE PRE-CLOSURE INVARIANT AS A PASSING CHECK.** `demos/village/phase-membership.mjs:68` asserts `version.members.length === members.length` (freeze/select cardinality equality) and `:103` compares the frozen count against a live `select` count. Both hold today ONLY because the demo's scope happens to contain any operator-authored retraction already. Add one retraction the scope excludes and MEMBER.1b flips RED for a CORRECT system. Rewrite both against the READING rather than the count.

(3) **`freeze` IS NOT ROUTED THROUGH THE NARROWING HELPERS.** `test/gateway/narrowing.ts:5` names 'a frozen module version' as exactly this class and instructs every new narrowing operation to use `assertPreservesSuppression` / `assertClosureDoesNotLeak`. `freeze`'s bespoke rail (`container-identity.test.ts:233`) asserts SET MEMBERSHIP — which `narrowing.ts:13-17` explicitly calls the weaker rail 'that passed while the bug shipped'. There is no leak rail and no transitivity rail for freeze. Route it through both.
tickets.title: freeze now exceeds select, and neither the spec nor the village was told

tickets.body:
STATUS 2026-07-21: items (2) and (3) are DONE on branch fix/t42-lens-name-gate (PR 155). What follows replaces the original body, whose factual claims that same branch invalidated.

DONE: .adlc/ now holds findings.jsonl (29 entries - live prosecutor findings plus H1-H7 backfilled, each marked as a backfill on the record) and manifest.jsonl (hash-chained, verify exits 0). .gitignore was ignoring .adlc/* so both would have been invisible to git and evaporated at the next clone - fixed with explicit negations. NOTE the reviewer recommendation not yet taken: the negation list is deny-by-default, so any evidence file a future adlc version writes is silently untracked on creation. Inverting it to ignore only known-scratch paths (.adlc/tmp/) would make new durable artifacts tracked by default. That is the same machinery-switched-off shape this ticket is about, so it belongs here.

DONE: T42 is the first ticket in this repo ever to declare rails, written through adlc ticket update hash-guarded path. rails-guard now ENGAGES (it exits 2 on that branch, correctly - rails authored on the same branch read as rail-edits against the merge-base, and no suppression exists for rail-edit).

REMAINING, and the reason this ticket stays open:

(a) RAILS ON THE REST OF THE BACKLOG. Only T42 declares rails. Do NOT backfill them retroactively - a rail written by the author of already-merged code shares that code premise, which is exactly how three hollow rails shipped on 2026-07-21. Instead: freeze rails BEFORE the build on every new ticket, which is P3 stated order and has never once been followed here. That single change is the structural fix; backfill is at best cleanup.

(b) A rail-authored-on-branch SUPPRESSION for rails-guard, so the honest first-branch case passes cleanly and ADLC_RAILS_BYPASS stays rare enough to be worth scrutinising. Today the legitimate case and the illegitimate one are indistinguishable.

(c) BYPASS ATTESTATION. A rails-bypass manifest entry currently carries a free-text why written by the bypassing party. It should carry the finding ids or prosecutor run that justify it, hash-linked.

(d) The backfilled H1-H7 ledger entries were filed against hand-picked files, and at least two do not exhibit the hazard they record (H7 against store/sqlite.ts, whose real sites are publish and promote/adopt.ts; H3 and H5 both against lifecycle.ts as a stand-in). P7 distill clusters by recurrence, so a hazard filed at line 1 of an innocent module either fails to cluster or manufactures a false one. Re-point them, or mark them structurally synthetic with a backfill flag rather than a note buried in prose.
tickets.title: Turn on the ADLC machinery we have never used: rails, findings ledger, gate evidence

tickets.body:
SPLIT OUT OF T47 on 2026-07-21. It shared nothing with T47 but the audit that found it; T47 became the public-pin chain and this is the translator guard. STILL PLAUSIBLE - probe first, and note the recommendation at the end is to REUSE promotionRefusal rather than write a second guard.

`translate` RE-SPEAKS STRANGER CONTENT IN THE OPERATOR'S VOICE WITH NO RESERVED-VOCABULARY GUARD.** `applyTemplate` (`src/federation/translate.ts:210-236`) builds each emitted pointer as `{kind: 'entity', entity: {id: src.target.entity.id, context: p.context!}}` - **the entity id comes from the recognized, possibly hostile delta**; primitive holes copy `src.target.value` verbatim. `parseEmitTemplate` (:55-97) accepts any non-empty string as a context and any primitive as a value, with no reserved-namespace check. Compare `promotionRefusal` (`adopt.ts:66-85`), which guards THE SAME CROSSING - foreign content becoming the operator's own claim - by refusing `loam.*`/`rhizomatic.*` contexts, `loam:` ids, and negations, with the comment 'operator authorship is exactly what gives a delta force here'. The translator performs that identical crossing ungated. And the crossing really is into the operator's voice in the reference deployment: `demos/village/village.mjs:297`, `phase9.mjs:87,114`, `demos/tutorial/lessons.mjs:945` all call `translate(gw, { seed: almanac.seed })` with the OPERATOR seed, so emissions land inside `lawfulSnapshot` where `readRegistrations`/`readTrustPolicy`/`readPublicSchemas`/`readBudgetPolicy`/`readBindingDefinitions`/`survivingTombstones` read them as LAW. Weaponizing needs the operator to have blessed a spec whose template names a reserved context, at which point the stranger picks the entity id (`loam:store`, `loam:public`, `loam:trust`) and the primitive values (a grant's `subject`) - an operator FOOTGUN rather than a stranger-only path, which is why it is PLAUSIBLE. But it is exactly the footgun `promotionRefusal` exists to remove, and the asymmetry between the two doors looks unintended rather than reasoned. PROBE: a template naming a reserved context, emitted under the operator seed - does it bind as law? RECOMMENDATION if confirmed: reuse `promotionRefusal` rather than writing a second guard.


CARE: this crossing is foreign content becoming the operator own claim, the same crossing promotionRefusal guards. If the probe confirms, the fix is a repair of an unintended asymmetry; if it turns out the asymmetry was reasoned, that is a design question and Myk decides.
tickets.title: translate re-speaks stranger content in the operator voice with no reserved-vocabulary guard

tickets.body:
HISTORICAL AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle H6. CONFIRMED mechanism. Hazard H6 at the MINT site rather than at a door.

registrationDeltaClaims treats its second argument as the LENS name - it stamps the Schema and derives the LIVING entity schema:<name>, which is exactly what lensNameOf slices back out on replay to become Registration.lensName, the key every door uses through lensOf.
