Both other mint paths pass the lens: publishRegistrationImpl computes schema.name ?? hyperschema.name, registerImpl the same. assembleGenesis ALONE passes reg.hyperschema.name - the PROGRAM - and it silently discards Registration.lensName, a field the very input type it accepts declares for this purpose.

CONSEQUENCE, live under 21.7 coexistence: a genesis declaring two readings over one hyperschema mints BOTH at living entity schema:<program>, both re-stamped with the program name. On replay readRegistrations keys latest-per-lens by [registrationEntity, lensName]; both share BOTH components, so the LAST entry in the array wins and the other vanishes with no error. Whichever survives serves under the program name - and if the operator public declaration names that program name (the natural name, and the only one genesis can produce), the anonymous surface admits it. ARRAY ORDER, not the operator declared reading, decides whether the redacted or the full policy sits behind the anonymous door.

NOTE ON HOW THIS WAS MISSED: test/surface/rest-lens-gate.test.ts documented this as an unavoidable fixture constraint (genesis derives that from the hyperschema, so a genesis lens can never differ) and routed its readings through publishRegistration to work around it. A constraint accepted rather than questioned. Worth remembering as its own lesson.

FIX: pass the lens - reg.lensName ?? reg.schema.name ?? reg.hyperschema.name. AND make the collapse loud: assembleGenesis should REFUSE a spec in which two registrations over the same hyperschema entity resolve to the same lens name, since that pair can never both bind.

RAILS - BOTH LEVELS. Delta level: a genesis naming two readings mints two DISTINCT living entities, and both survive readRegistrations. Object level: each lens answers under its own policy at a door, and a public declaration opens exactly the declared one. Plus the refusal rail: a genesis whose two registrations collapse to one lens name is refused, not silently reduced.
tickets.title: assembleGenesis mints every lens under the PROGRAM name — sibling readings collapse silently, and array order picks what the anonymous door serves

tickets.body:
HISTORICAL AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle H1. CONFIRMED. Hazard H1 meeting SPEC 25 row quarantine - a combination neither ticket anticipated.

deltasSince sets aside any row failing admit (unparseable, id-mismatch, invalid-signature) and reads on. That is a NARROWING of the delta-set, and it is the one narrowing in the tree that CANNOT carry a negation closure, because the dropped row is precisely the thing that is illegible. The driver own justification is the H1 blind spot verbatim: the bad row contributes to no view, exactly as a not-yet-synced delta contributes to none. A missing CLAIM contributes nothing. A missing NEGATION REVIVES ITS TARGET.

SCENARIO: a tenant author retracts a claim through clearEntity/retract - the negation lands as its own row. Later that row bytes are torn by a half-written batch (the exact case the quarantine module names). On the next boot the reactor replays only admissible deltas, negated ranges over a ground that no longer holds the strike, and EVERY reader resolves the retracted value as live: GraphQL, the anonymous door, and every federation peer pulling offeredDeltas. The same shape restores write standing to a REVOKED GRANT (revocation is a plain negation) and drops a TOMBSTONE out of readTombstones, letting a forgotten id back through the federation door.

AGGRAVATOR - IT IS NOT SELF-HEALING. The deltas table declares id UNIQUE and appends with INSERT OR IGNORE. A corrupt row squats on the id, so heal replanting the archive healthy copy of the same negation is SILENTLY IGNORED (changes is 0, so the report honestly says 0 and the operator sees nothing). MirrorBackend.deltasSince delegates to the primary only, so the archive good copy is never read either. repair re-admit re-runs admission on the same bad bytes and fails again. The revival is DURABLE until a human runs repair discard and re-federates.

AND THE OPERATOR IS NOT TOLD. loam repair list prints key, reason, preview - nothing says a retraction fell out. Two of the three reasons (id-mismatch, invalid-signature) have ALREADY parsed their claims, so the driver can see the row carries a negates ref and can name the target. Only unparseable is genuinely opaque, and even there the operator can be warned that strikes may be missing.

FIX: give QuarantinedRow an optional negates field, set wherever admit reached parsed-claims, and have repair list say plainly that this row struck an id and that claim is live again until settled. For unparseable, emit a standing warning that the ground may be missing strikes. Separately make repair able to REPLACE a quarantined row bytes with a healthy copy of the same id - an INSERT OR REPLACE reserved to repair re-admit --from-mirror, or a discard-then-heal step in heal - so a corrupt negation is not permanently unrecoverable behind INSERT OR IGNORE.

RAILS - BOTH LEVELS, and USE test/gateway/narrowing.ts assertPreservesSuppression (which currently has ZERO callers - see the hollow-rail finding). Strike a claim, corrupt the negation row, boot, assert the claim does NOT read live through a Schema, and assert the operator is told which target lost its strike.

CARE: this is a REPAIR of stated behavior. local-storage.ts:159-179 has the identical shape.
tickets.title: A quarantined row that is a NEGATION revives its target — durably, because INSERT OR IGNORE blocks the heal

tickets.body:
HISTORICAL AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle H1. TWO CONFIRMED findings in one function. Sibling of T54 (which is the same file, different defect - the reserved-vocabulary guard).

(1) SUPPRESSION IS NOT TRANSITIVE ACROSS translates. translate re-expresses a source delta as a NEW delta signed by the translator, citing the source with a translates pointer. That is a COPY of a claim out of the ground into a fresh id, and the negation closure is checked only ONCE, at emit time. After the pass nothing links the two: translates is read in exactly one place in src/ - the terminal-role check - and by nothing that resolves, masks, or negates.

Scenario: a peer delta is recognized and translated on Monday. On Tuesday the operator lawfully strikes the source. The source is suppressed for every reader. The EMISSION is a different id, authored by the translator, with no negation pointing at it - so the canonical lens still resolves the retired fact and serves it to GraphQL, the anonymous door, and every peer pulling offeredDeltas (whose closure carries only negations OF admitted deltas, and there is no negation of the emission to carry). RE-RUNNING translate DOES NOT REPAIR IT: the pass only adds, the emission is already present, union swallows the re-run, and the stale copy is never struck. This is the H1 family at its most durable - the narrowing is a RE-ASSERTION rather than a filter, so the retraction can never reach the copy at all.

FIX: make each pass a RECONCILIATION rather than an append-only emit - for every held emission whose translates source is now struck, sign a negation of the emission (grow-only, deterministic timestamp so it stays idempotent). The alternative (teach canonical readers to treat an emission as struck when its source is) is NOT preferred: peers cannot run Loam reader rule, so the property must be visible in the deltas.

(2) THE STRUCK-SOURCE GATE USES CONSTITUTIONAL STANDING FOR A DATA QUESTION. lawfulNegated counts a negation only when its author IS THE OPERATOR. That is right for LAW - a foreign strike must not retire the operator schema - and wrong for DATA, which is what translate is filtering. The governed data gather honors a wider set: the operator OR any author named subject by a surviving operator-minted grant.

Scenario: a granted tenant retracts their own claim via clearEntity - the negation is signed by the TENANT. Every governed reader now sees it struck. The next translate pass evaluates the source under operator-only standing, sees it surviving, and mints a fresh canonical delta carrying the retracted content - signed by the translator, so it is not even the tenant to retract. The tenant own retraction is silently undone in the canonical lens, and the copy federates outward.

FIX: evaluate the source through the same trust-masked ground the governed gather uses (a shared dataStruck helper built on lawfulStrikersJson), so what translate re-renders and what a reader resolves cannot drift.

RAILS - BOTH LEVELS. For (1): strike a source AFTER a pass, assert reactor.negationsOf(emission) is non-empty AND the canonical View no longer carries the value. For (2): grantee retracts, run translate, assert no emission cites the retracted source AND the canonical View is empty.
tickets.title: Suppression never follows the translates edge — a retraction after the pass leaves the canonical copy live forever

tickets.body:
HISTORICAL AUDIT 2026-07-21, angle H7. Both CONFIRMED, both recorded to the ledger and not queued until the PR-155 panel flagged the gap.

(1) PULL REPORTS A POST-DROP COUNT AS THE OFFER. pullFrom (src/federation/pull.ts:64-73) reconstructs each wire delta in a try/catch and drops failures on the floor. The array handed to federate is therefore ALREADY filtered, and federateImpl computes offered from what it was GIVEN, not from what the peer sent; rejected counts only verification and admission failures inside federate. A peer offering 100 deltas of which 40 will not reconstruct yields the report offered 60, accepted 60, rejected 0. There is no field, no log, and no return value anywhere carrying the 40.

The DROP is sanctioned by the deliberate-divergence note in offer.ts (a live peer stream may be partially good, and the next pull heals). Erasing the drop from the REPORT is not. The comment in pull.ts - federate counts what it admits - is precisely the mistaken inference: federate cannot count what never reached it. And because the failure is a property of the peer bytes rather than of timing, the next pull heals is FALSE here: every subsequent pull drops the same 40 and prints the same clean line.

FIX: count the drops in the catch and surface them. Either fold them into rejected and set offered to body.deltas.length so that offered equals accepted plus rejected becomes a checkable invariant, or widen FederationReport with an explicit unreconstructable field. cmdPull should name it.

(2) THE RUNNER PARTITION IS NOT A PARTITION. Runner.attach (src/runner/runner.ts:141-157) returns installed and skipped, documented as the bindings it could run and the ones whose implementation it lacks - a pair that reads as covering every binding the store holds. It does not: readBindingDefinitions drops candidates BEFORE attach sees them (a mistyped field hits a type-gate continue, an unparseable emit payload hits catch continue), so those names appear in neither list and installed plus skipped is less than the number of binding definitions in the ground. A deploy check of the form skipped is empty therefore passes while a typo-d definition sits inert, computing nothing. The comment at the drop site is correct about blast radius and silent about reporting.

FIX: have readBindingDefinitions report its drops - return the specs alongside a malformed list of deltaId and reason, or accept an onMalformed sink - and surface them as a third list on Runner, so the three together account for every binding delta in the ground.

RAILS - BOTH LEVELS for each. For (1): feed pullFrom an injected fetch returning a mix of good and corrupt wire deltas, assert the report accounts for EVERY delta the peer sent, and assert at the delta level which ones landed. For (2): plant a definition with an unparseable emit and assert attach NAMES it rather than omitting it.

CARE: both are REPAIRS of a reporting promise. Neither changes what the system serves.
tickets.title: Two completeness misreports with no ticket: federation pull hides unreconstructable deltas, and the runner drops malformed bindings from its accounting

tickets.body:
Myk, 2026-07-21: comments are too verbose, and much of what they carry is process history rather than explanation. That churns tokens on every read and leaves legacy cruft crowding the real signal. CLAUDE.md now carries the rule (Comments explain the code; HISTORY goes in the journal). This ticket applies it to what is already written.

THE TEST, applied file by file: if a comment names a PR, an audit, an earlier draft, or a person, it is history and belongs in JOURNAL.md, the commit message, or .adlc/findings.jsonl. If it explains what will bite whoever changes this code next - a non-obvious substrate behavior, an invariant, why not the obvious thing - it stays.

MEASURED STARTING POINT (2026-07-21): the three files written that day run 33 percent comment lines. test/gateway/byte-door-lens-gate.test.ts was brought to 21 percent as the worked example; use its header as the target shape. Ten instances of explicit process narration were counted across src/ and test/ by grepping for phrases like an audit, a prosecutor, the first draft, was found hollow, caught my own, and PR numbers.

KNOWN OFFENDERS, all authored 2026-07-21:
- test/surface/rest-lens-gate.test.ts - the header narrates what an audit corrected and what an earlier draft got wrong. Keep only: what it rails, and that it does NOT rail servesLive and cannot.
- scripts/patch-adlc-npx.mjs - 48 comment lines including a paragraph about a prosecutor pass and a paragraph about the author walking into the trap the header describes. Both are journal material. Keep the technical why: node against npx-cli.js rather than shell true, and what shell true would do to argv.
- Assorted headers that restate hazards SUBSTRATE-HAZARDS.md already owns. Cite the hazard id in one line instead of re-explaining it.

DO NOT over-correct. The repo rule that errors, help text and docs are first-class craft still stands, and a comment carrying a genuinely non-obvious substrate fact is worth its lines - the resolveView backfill note in the byte-door rail is the example of one to KEEP. The target is removing archaeology, not removing explanation.

CARE: pure comment churn on frozen rails will trip rails-guard. Do it as its own PR so the diff is reviewable as text-only, and expect the rail-edit violations - they are legitimate here and the bypass should say so.
tickets.title: Strip process narration from source comments — history belongs in the journal, not in every read

tickets.body:
PROSECUTOR FINDINGS on the T55 diff, deferred rather than folded in so the fix stayed reviewable. Both CONFIRMED.

(1) THE REPLICA PATH DECIDES NOTHING. eraseImpl now decides completeness from BYTE PRESENCE after sweeping every tier - a purge count of 0 is ambiguous, since it means never held it exactly as often as failed to remove it. eraseReplicaImpl still discards its purge count entirely, so a pool holding the byte and removing nothing yields a top level erase that reports completion for content still at rest inside the operator own walls. That is precisely the 24.8 evasion channel the comment above that function says must not exist. Fix: give both sites ONE rule - after purge and reseat, assert the id is absent from the backend own listing. That answer is correct for a pool that never held the id (absent, complete) and for a tier a previous attempt already cleared (absent, complete), and still fails loudly for the read only mount.

(2) THE ARCHIVE SWEEP IS NOW ON THE BOOT PATH AND UNBOUNDED. heal purges unconditionally with the whole accumulated tombstone set, and it runs on boot. ArchiveBackend.purge loops ids crossed with fan directories: an existence check per pair, plus a prefix scan of that fan whole file list per pair. Its own comment budgets roughly 256000 directory reads per heal as the thing to avoid; the readdir was hoisted but the per pair work was not. With a thousand historical erasures and ten thousand archived deltas that is a quarter million stat calls and millions of string comparisons added to every boot, growing monotonically and never reclaimable because tombstones are append only. Fix: invert to a SINGLE pass over the fan listings testing membership in a Set of dead ids, and consider a persisted swept high water mark so a steady state boot sweeps only the newly dead.

RAILS: for (1) a pool that holds the byte and refuses to remove it must make the top level erase refuse, and a pool that never held it must not. For (2) a store with N tombstones and M archived deltas must not do work proportional to N times M on boot - assert the call counts, not the wall clock.

CARE: both are REPAIRS. (2) is a performance regression introduced by T55 itself, so it should not sit long.
tickets.title: Erasure completeness is decided in two places by two different rules — unify on byte-presence, and bound the archive sweep

tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE (P6 is Myk's). Drafted from a design conversation with Myk on 2026-07-21. The deliverable is the SPEC §29 prose (spec/29-slating-and-graveyards.md — §29 is the next free section; §28 container-trust is the last one written, and reserved numbers are load-bearing so do not renumber) plus answers to the open questions below — then STOP for his word before any implementation code.

THE PROBLEM. Erasure is the operator's alone (SPEC §11), which is correct. But the operator reaches across tenant (§7) and container (§24/§27) boundaries, so an erasure can invalidate data a tenant is relying on, with no warning at all.

WHAT WE REJECTED, AND WHY IT MATTERS. The obvious answer is a dry-run that reports impact. It is wrong, and the reason generalizes: a dry-run list is an OBSERVATION of a world that keeps moving, so it drifts, and a notification built on a stale list under-reports silently. That is hazard H8 exactly — an index of data location fails OPEN. Caching the dry-run result makes it worse, not better.

THE DESIGN. Not a dry-run. ONE operation in TWO PHASES: identify, then erase. Identification SLATES the deltas, and a slate is a CONTAINER (the §27 primitive; T32 lifts quarantine into the general form, so this is a PRESET, not new machinery). The slate does not merely record the impacted set — it CLOSES PROPAGATION, so the set cannot grow after identification. The notification list is then true by ENFORCEMENT rather than by observation. That is the whole move.

THE LATTICE, and it is the spine of the prose. Myk's observation: READING IS A KIND OF DEPENDENCY — a view depends on the presence of the delta. That collapses what looked like two options into one model. A slate closes some subset of three doors:

  - EGRESS (offer / federation-out): the holder set cannot grow. This is what makes the notification true.
  - CITE (append door + federation-in door): the dependent set cannot grow, so no new orphans exist at cut time.
  - READ (GraphQL, REST, byte-door, public): the data is already effectively gone; the bytes are a formality.

"Still temporarily available" is precisely NOT closing read. So a slate is a membership set plus WHICH DOORS HONOUR IT — which is probably what container policy IS in general. DECIDED (Myk): this is CONFIGURATION, not a fixed policy, because the right point differs by context. A slate that closes read is nearly an erasure already; one that leaves read open is a grace period. Same machinery, different legal posture.

MINTING VS ADMISSION — the nuance that makes closing CITE legitimate. A founding principle is that anyone can create any delta at any time, so refusing citations SOUNDS like a violation. It is not. The substrate cannot stop anyone MINTING anything (erase.ts states this verbatim about tombstones); policy lives at ADMISSION. A slate never touches anyone's ability to create a delta — it declines to persist it HERE. Consequence: this is a per-store policy, so a peer without the slate accepts those citations freely, and the warning reaches only parties writing INTO this store.

DIRECT CITATION ONLY, NOT TRANSITIVE. A direct check is a Set lookup at admission, the same cost class as the readTombstones check that already runs there. Transitive closure is the unbounded scan H8 warns about. Draw the line deliberately rather than discovering it later.

THE DISCLOSURE ASYMMETRY, which must be in the spec. At the APPEND door a refusal can be informative, because the only parties who can trigger it are parties who could already read the target — and telling them IS the notification. The mechanism and the warning turn out to be the same thing. At the FEDERATION-IN door that reasoning FAILS: a peer pushing a delta citing a slated id may have no read access to it, so a distinguishable refusal would tell them something exists and is on its way out. The federation door needs the uniform-refusal discipline (§12/§13); the append door does not.

ONE FUNCTION, TWO DOORS. The cite-check has two admission sites (append authorize, federation inbound). All seven findings of 2026-07-21 were one-rule-N-sites-one-drifts, and the federation site is the one that drifts — it always is. Specify a single shared predicate, not two checks that happen to agree today.

THE GRAVEYARD. Dropping the slate is the LAST act of erasure — the container's destruction is the commit point, which beats a flag someone must remember to clear. The record that survives is a GRAVEYARD (the erasure EVENT: which erasure, what reason, who was notified, what window, which ids) rather than only per-id tombstones. Two constraints:

  - Its membership must be FROZEN, not queried (Gateway.freeze materializes the member set). A queried membership empties itself the moment the deltas go, losing the record.
  - It CITES tombstones, it does not replace them. readTombstones is load-bearing at every door (admission refusal, the federation gate). If the graveyard also encoded "is this id refused" there would be two records of one fact — the exact failure pattern of 2026-07-21. Tombstones stay the per-id law; the graveyard is the narrative. It holds content addresses, and retaining a hash retains zero content.

ALSO IN SCOPE:
  - UN-SLATING. If the operator changes their mind mid-window, striking the slate is forgiveness before the cut — the same shape as striking a tombstone.
  - ERASURE REACHES THROUGH THE SLATE. §24.8 says a container that could hide a purged byte is an evasion channel. A slate IS a container, so a delta slated here and also sitting in a pool needs the cut to get both, and the slate must not become the hiding place. Slightly recursive; state it.

DECIDED BY MYK IN CHAT, 2026-07-21 — do not re-litigate:
  1. The §11 promise CHANGES. Erasure becomes two-phase with a visible intermediate state rather than one instantaneous act. His words: "this complies better with everyone's needs and expectations." A landing must update §11 accordingly.
  2. Propagation vs dependency is CONFIGURATION, not a fixed choice.
  3. WE ARE NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR FEDERATION. Erasure does not reach peers — they are not the operator's replicas — and we do not pretend otherwise. We can only NOTIFY. The feature must say this plainly rather than implying a reach it does not have.

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR MYK:
  Q1. THE WINDOW BOUND. How long may a slate sit before the cut? A compliance clock runs from the request ("without undue delay"), and a tenant can still read what a subject asked to have forgotten. This is the one number the model should not pick. Recommendation: a per-slate deadline with a store-level maximum, defaulting short, and a slate past its deadline closing READ rather than silently persisting.
  Q2. NOTIFICATION CONTENT. What may a tenant actually be told? Naming the delta tells them what is going; naming nothing is useless. Recommendation: identify the CONTAINER and the affected entity/lens rather than the subject or the reason, so a tenant learns what will change in their view without learning whose request caused it.
  Q3. Does the slate get its own vocabulary or reuse the quarantine-pool vocabulary? Recommendation: its own preset name under the container vocabulary once T32 lands, so a reader can never confuse a staging area (things coming IN to canon) with a slate (things going OUT). The edges point in opposite directions even though membership works the same.

GROUNDED FACT, checked 2026-07-21 rather than assumed: admission today refuses a delta whose OWN id is dead (ingest.ts, `dead.has(d.id)`) and does NOT inspect pointers. So a delta CITING an erased id is admitted today, and a citation refused during a slate could simply be resubmitted after the cut, landing as a dangling reference. The design must say whether that is intended.

RAILS (when it eventually builds) — BOTH LEVELS. Delta level: the slate's membership is frozen, and the graveyard survives the cut carrying the ids. Object level: for each door-closure configuration, what a reader RESOLVES and what a door ANSWERS for a slated delta — including that a closed-read slate serves the same uniform refusal as an absent delta, and that the federation-in cite refusal is indistinguishable from any other federation refusal.

CARE: DESIGN-STAGE. Draft spec/29-slating-and-graveyards.md and answer Q1-Q3 with reasoned recommendations, then STOP and wait for Myk in chat. It CHANGES WHAT THE SYSTEM PROMISES (§11), so it is his merge regardless.

edges.contract: T32 lifts quarantine into the general Container primitive. The slate is a PRESET over it — a membership set plus which doors honour it — so building against quarantine pools now means rebuilding after T32.

tickets.title: Two-phase erasure: a slate is a container, and dropping it is the cut

tickets.body:
SPLIT FROM T57 during the overnight churn (2026-07-22). T57 bundled two safety improvements for the "a quarantined negation revives its target" bug; the DISCLOSURE half (the operator is told a strike fell out) landed as T57. This is the RECOVERY half, split off because it touches delicate INSERT/heal semantics across two drivers and warrants its own careful pass — the T55 lesson: a naive fix on erasure-critical recovery code can be worse than the bug.

THE REMAINING BUG. A corrupt negation row squats on its id. The deltas table declares `id UNIQUE` and append uses `INSERT OR IGNORE`, so `heal` replanting the archive's HEALTHY copy of that same negation is silently ignored (changes === 0). `MirrorBackend.deltasSince` delegates to the primary only, so the archive's good copy is never read on a normal boot either. `repair re-admit` re-runs admission on the same bad bytes and fails again. So the revival is DURABLE until a human runs `repair discard` + re-federate. After T57 the operator is at least TOLD (the negates field + repair-list warning), but recovery is still manual.

THE FIX (design choice to settle): make a corrupt row REPLACEABLE by a healthy copy of the same id. Two candidate shapes named in the original T57 body:
  (a) an INSERT OR REPLACE reserved to a new `repair re-admit --from-mirror <key>` verb — the operator explicitly pulls the healthy copy from the archive/mirror over the corrupt primary row, or
  (b) a discard-then-heal step folded into `heal` itself — when heal has a valid delta from the archive whose id is currently QUARANTINED in the primary, it replaces the corrupt row.

SAFETY CONSTRAINT (the delicate part): a replace must NOT let a wrong/malicious delta overwrite a good one. The invariant: replace only when (i) the incoming delta ADMITS (parses, id matches, signature valid) AND (ii) the existing primary row for that id is currently QUARANTINED (failed admission). Never replace an admitted row. This check has to run inside the append/heal path with visibility into quarantine status, which is why it is more than a one-line change.

local-storage.ts has the identical INSERT-OR-IGNORE-equivalent shape and needs the same treatment.

RAILS — BOTH LEVELS, and USE test/gateway/narrowing.ts `assertPreservesSuppression`. Strike a claim, corrupt the negation row in the primary, seed the HEALTHY copy in the mirror/archive, run heal (or repair re-admit --from-mirror), then: object level — the claim does NOT read live through a Schema (the strike is restored); delta level — the primary now holds the healthy negation bytes, and no admitted row was overwritten (a sibling healthy claim survives untouched). Add the negative rail: an admitted row is NEVER replaced by a re-federated delta.

CARE: REPAIR of stated §11/§25 behavior, but it touches heal + INSERT semantics on erasure-critical paths — prosecute hard, and consider whether Myk should eye the replace-invariant before merge (an over-broad replace is an erasure-integrity hazard).
tickets.title: Recovery for a corrupt negation row: heal must be able to REPLACE it from a healthy copy (split from T57)
